---
title: "Methodology"
---


::: {.callout-note icon=false}
## Plain-Language Summary

This section describes the system end-to-end: how a question becomes
a set of retrieved document chunks, how those chunks become a cited
answer, and how the citations and the refusal are checked before
either is shown to the user. Two design decisions are central. First,
the system uses **two** retrieval methods at once (one based on the
meaning of words, one based on the exact words used) and combines
their results, because each method handles a kind of query the other
handles poorly. Second, the system enforces its citation and refusal
*contracts* in code — they are not relied on as instructions the
language model is asked to follow. A reader who only wants the
architectural picture can read the *Pipeline Overview* sub-section
below and skip the rest.
:::

---

## Pipeline Overview {#sec-method-overview}

Cedant is a six-stage pipeline whose stages are intentionally
decoupled. Each stage has a single responsibility, lives in its own
module, and is independently testable. The stages are:

```
       ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
       │                                                          │
       │   USER QUESTION                                          │
       │        │                                                 │
       │        ▼                                                 │
       │  ┌─────────────────┐                                     │
       │  │ Hybrid          │  Dense (BGE-M3) + Sparse (BM25)     │
       │  │ Retrieval       │  fused via Reciprocal Rank Fusion   │
       │  │                 │  in Qdrant. Top-k = 5 by default.   │
       │  └────────┬────────┘                                     │
       │           │                                              │
       │           ▼                                              │
       │  ┌─────────────────┐                                     │
       │  │ Prompt          │  System prompt (frozen v2) +        │
       │  │ Assembly        │  retrieved SOURCES block +          │
       │  │                 │  user QUESTION.                     │
       │  └────────┬────────┘                                     │
       │           │                                              │
       │           ▼                                              │
       │  ┌─────────────────┐                                     │
       │  │ LLM Call        │  Local oMLX, OpenAI-compatible      │
       │  │ (temperature=0) │  chat-completions. Default          │
       │  │                 │  Gemma 4 31B IT (D015).             │
       │  └────────┬────────┘                                     │
       │           │                                              │
       │           ▼                                              │
       │  ┌─────────────────┐                                     │
       │  │ Citation        │  Regex-extract every [chunk_id];    │
       │  │ Validation      │  partition into valid /             │
       │  │                 │  hallucinated against retrieved set.│
       │  └────────┬────────┘                                     │
       │           │                                              │
       │           ▼                                              │
       │  ┌─────────────────┐                                     │
       │  │ Refusal         │  Exact-phrase strict match;         │
       │  │ Detector        │  partial refusals rejected.         │
       │  └────────┬────────┘                                     │
       │           │                                              │
       │           ▼                                              │
       │  ┌─────────────────┐                                     │
       │  │ AnswerResult    │  Structured object: answer text,    │
       │  │                 │  citations[], hallucinated[],       │
       │  │                 │  used_chunks[], refused, elapsed.   │
       │  └─────────────────┘                                     │
       │                                                          │
       └──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

Each stage is described in detail below. The two contracts (citation
and refusal) are validated after the LLM call, not relied on as
prompt instructions; if the LLM ignores the prompt, the contract
detectors still fire correctly.

---

## Hybrid Retrieval {#sec-method-retrieval}

The retrieval layer was committed as D009 after a Day-2 path-selection
review. Two channels run in parallel against the indexed corpus, and
their results are fused at the storage layer.

### Why hybrid, not dense alone

Three properties of the corpus make pure-dense retrieval an
incomplete answer:

- **Named entities matter for citation.** Specific regulation
  identifiers (`SS5/25`, `EIOPA-BoS-14/253`), paragraph numbers
  ("4.124"), and defined terms (`ORSA`, `ICAAP`) carry semantic
  weight that dense embeddings often dilute against surrounding
  prose. BM25 surfaces these by exact match.

- **Free-form descriptive prose dominates the rest.** Most of each
  document is descriptive language where dense embeddings'
  generalisation strength is the right tool. BM25 alone would miss
  paraphrased queries.

- **The two failure modes are different and complementary.** A dense
  miss is typically a vocabulary mismatch ("greenhouse gas exposures"
  vs "GHG"); a sparse miss is typically a topic-shift query
  ("guidance on third-party providers" should match SS1/21 even if
  it uses different terminology). Fusion mitigates both.

A pure-dense baseline was the obvious alternative; it was rejected on
Day-2 evidence after a hand-trial of three queries failed on
named-entity recall.

### Dense channel: BGE-M3 via mlx-embeddings (D009 + D010)

The dense channel uses `BAAI/bge-m3` (1024-dimensional embeddings),
served locally on Apple Silicon via `mlx-embeddings`. The choice of
BGE-M3 reflects its strong domain coverage on regulatory English and
its widespread use as a hybrid-retrieval baseline; the choice of MLX
over PyTorch reflects the project's local-first / Apple-Silicon-
native posture established by D004 and reinforced throughout the
stack.

A specific pooling decision was committed as **D010**: dense
embeddings use the **CLS token output, L2-normalised**, not the
`text_embeds` mean-pooled convenience output. The `mlx-embeddings`
library's default `text_embeds` is mean-pooled across all token
positions; BGE-M3 was trained with CLS-pooled representations, and
using the default produces representations that are systematically
off-distribution from the model's intended use. D010 was prompted by
a Day-2 retrieval failure on a query that should have hit; the fix
was a one-line change but the diagnostic that found it is recorded
in the decision log.

### What was rejected from BGE-M3's full feature set

BGE-M3 produces three vector types from a single forward pass: dense,
learned sparse, and ColBERT (late-interaction multi-vector). The
canonical implementation is `FlagEmbedding`, which runs on PyTorch.

The full multi-functionality was **not** used at v1, for two reasons:

- The `mlx-embeddings` and `mlx-embedding-models` packages load
  BGE-M3 as a standard XLM-RoBERTa encoder. The sparse linear+ReLU
  head and ColBERT projection head are model-specific layers not
  implemented in either MLX package. Going via MLX therefore means
  **dense-only**.
- The alternatives — running BGE-M3 under PyTorch via FlagEmbedding,
  or under ONNX — both break the MLX-everywhere stack and add
  dependency surface that is hard to justify against a initial
  budget.

BM25 substitutes for BGE-M3's learned sparse head. At a 461-chunk
corpus, the expected quality gap between BGE-M3's trained sparse
weights and Porter-stemmed BM25 is small; D009 explicitly accepts
this trade-off. ColBERT-style late-interaction reranking is recorded
as a v2 option under **Q7**, to be revisited if the v1 evaluation
reveals retrieval ceiling effects that the v1 channels cannot
address. (The N=70 results in Section 6 show that retrieval *is*
the dominant bottleneck — 25% of answerable questions fail at the
retrieval stage — so Q7 is a real v2 candidate, not a placeholder.)

### Sparse channel: BM25 with Porter stemming (D011)

The sparse channel is **BM25** over a custom corpus-wide vocabulary,
committed as D011. The specific design choices:

- **Word-level tokenisation.** Subword tokenisation (WordPiece, BPE)
  was considered and rejected because BM25's scoring relies on
  meaningful term frequencies; subword splits produce fragment
  statistics that don't represent the underlying terminology.
- **Porter stemming.** Standard rule-based suffix-stripping. Maps
  "regulation" / "regulations" / "regulatory" to a shared stem,
  improving recall on inflected variants without requiring a
  trained morphological model.
- **Minimal stopwords.** A small set of high-frequency function
  words (the, a, of, in, ...) is filtered. Domain-specific
  near-stopwords are preserved because in regulatory text they
  often carry meaning (e.g. "shall" vs "may" vs "should").
- **Corpus-wide vocabulary.** Vocabulary is computed once at index
  time over the full corpus and persisted at
  `corpus/bm25_vocab.json` (~4,810 terms). Query-time tokenisation
  uses the same vocabulary; out-of-vocabulary query terms are
  silently dropped (the BM25 score for an OOV term is zero by
  construction).
- **BM25 parameters.** `k1 = 1.5`, `b = 0.75` — the rank-bm25
  defaults. Tuning was deferred until the eval suggested a
  tuning-specific failure mode; N=70 results do not show one.

### Indexing: Qdrant native sparse vectors (D012)

Both channels are indexed in a single Qdrant collection at
`scratch/qdrant/`, with the dense vector stored as the primary
1024-dim vector and the sparse vector stored as Qdrant's native
sparse-vector type (`(indices[], values[])` pairs). Qdrant has
supported sparse vectors as a first-class type since v1.7, which
allows the hybrid query to run server-side rather than requiring
client-side fusion against two separate stores.

The decision to use one storage system rather than two (Qdrant for
dense, an external BM25 store like Lucene for sparse) was D012, on
the basis that one operational concern is simpler than two for a
local-first artefact, and that Qdrant's sparse-vector support is
mature enough that the abstraction does not leak.

The full corpus payload (every chunk's text plus its hand-curated
metadata) is stored in the Qdrant point payload, so a retrieval
result is fully self-describing — there is no secondary store the
retriever has to consult to materialise the chunk text or its
provenance.

### Fusion: Reciprocal Rank Fusion (RRF)

The dense and sparse rankings are fused by **Reciprocal Rank
Fusion** at query time, via Qdrant's hybrid-query API. The fusion
formula is the standard:

$$
\text{RRF}(d) = \sum_{c \in \text{channels}} \frac{1}{k + r_c(d)}
$$

where $r_c(d)$ is the rank of document $d$ in channel $c$'s ranking,
and $k$ is a fusion constant. The system uses $k = 60$ (the
widely-cited Cormack et al. value) and pulls up to 50 candidates per
channel before fusion.

RRF was chosen over weighted score fusion because the two channels'
raw scores are on incomparable scales (cosine similarity for dense;
unbounded BM25 score for sparse); rank-based fusion sidesteps
score-normalisation entirely. The cost is that absolute confidence
information is lost — the fused score is a rank-derived value, not
a calibrated probability. The system does not currently expose
calibrated retrieval confidence to the answer model; Q3 in
`docs/open_questions.md` records the deferral.

### Filters applied at retrieval time

Two filters are applied to the retrieval candidate pool before
fusion:

- **`exclude_superseded`** (default: `True`) — drops chunks belonging
  to documents whose `superseded_by` field is non-null. In v1 this
  removes PRA SS3/19 chunks whenever its successor SS5/25 is
  available. Per D012, the default is on; users who specifically
  need the older perspective can override.
- **`issuer_type` / `jurisdiction`** (optional) — pass-through
  filters into Qdrant payload matching. The Streamlit interface
  exposes both as text inputs; the CLI exposes them as flags.

---

## Prompt Assembly and the LLM Call {#sec-method-llm}

The retrieved top-k chunks (default $k=5$) are assembled into a
SOURCES block and concatenated with the system prompt and the user
question. The LLM call is a standard OpenAI-compatible chat
completion against a local endpoint at `http://127.0.0.1:8000/v1`,
served by oMLX. Temperature is hard-pinned to **0.0** in
`_build_payload`, making the system deterministic for a given
(query, corpus, model, prompt) tuple.

### The production system prompt (v2)

The full production system prompt, frozen as the v2 version after
D014 and promoted to production by D015, lives at the top of
`src/underwriting_copilot/answer.py`:

```text
You are an underwriting copilot for a reinsurance company. You
answer questions about regulatory and corporate documents using
ONLY the sources provided in the user message. You never use
information from your training data.

Citation format:
- Every factual claim in your answer MUST be followed by a
  citation in square brackets containing the source's actual chunk
  identifier. Write the identifier verbatim from the SOURCES
  section, with NOTHING else inside the brackets — no prefix, no
  equals sign, no quotes, no abbreviation.
- Worked example: if the SOURCES section lists a source as
  [pra_ss5-25_climate__0027__role-of-scenario-analysis], cite a
  claim from it as [pra_ss5-25_climate__0027__role-of-scenario-analysis],
  not as [chunk_id=pra_ss5-25_climate__0027__role-of-scenario-analysis]
  and not as [chunk_id_1] or [source_1]. The brackets contain the
  verbatim identifier and nothing else.
- A single sentence may carry multiple citations back-to-back:
  [<first_identifier>][<second_identifier>].
- Use only identifiers that appear verbatim in the SOURCES
  section. Never invent an identifier.

Refusal rule:
- If the SOURCES do not contain enough information to answer the
  QUESTION, respond with EXACTLY this phrase and nothing else:

  I cannot answer this from the provided sources.

Keep answers concise. Quote source text only when exact wording
matters.
```

Three structural choices in this prompt deserve highlighting:

- **The `<ID>` worked example is load-bearing.** The Day-2
  observation that gave the system its sample query bank was a
  Qwen-vs-Gemma citation-format gap: under v1 of this prompt (which
  used the literal token `[chunk_id]` as both the placeholder name
  and the emit format), Qwen tended to drift to placeholders like
  `[chunk_id_1]` instead of substituting the actual identifier.
  The v2 prompt resolves the ambiguity by using the actual chunk-id
  format inside the worked example. The N=70 sweep shows the v2
  prompt closes most of the Qwen gap (Section 6).
- **"Use only identifiers that appear verbatim in the SOURCES
  section. Never invent an identifier."** This is the
  prompt-level statement of the citation contract. It is not relied
  on as the enforcement mechanism — the validator (below) catches
  fabricated identifiers regardless of whether the model honoured
  the instruction — but stating it explicitly reduces the rate at
  which models attempt to fabricate.
- **The refusal phrase appears verbatim in the prompt.** The
  detector matches on the same exact phrase. The prompt and the
  detector are one contract; changing either requires changing the
  other.

### Model selection: Gemma 4 31B IT (D015)

The production default is **`gemma-4-31B-it-MLX-6bit`**, selected
under D015 on the basis of the D014 evaluation, and strengthened by
the extended N=70 sweep. The specific properties that drove the
choice:

- **Citation discipline.** Gemma produces zero hallucinated citations
  across the full 88-cell answerable sweep (44 questions × 2
  prompts × Gemma). Qwen produces some on both prompts (23 on v1,
  7 on v2). Quantitatively small, qualitatively a behaviour
  difference.
- **Robustness to the prompt change.** Gemma's citation recall is
  identical under v1 and v2 (0.598 on both). Qwen's recall moves
  substantially (0.386 → 0.545). A model that is not prompt-fragile
  is preferred when the long-term maintainer of the system may not
  be the engineer who designed the prompt.
- **Refusal correctness.** Both models pass all 26 refusal questions
  across both prompts (104/104 cells correct). On this axis the
  choice is a wash.

The trade-off is **latency**. Gemma's mean answerable-query latency
is ~22 s; Qwen's is ~3 s. The slower latency is acceptable for
underwriting-research workflows (where the alternative is a human
spending several minutes finding the same paragraph), but a
deployment with stricter latency budgets can override the default
via the `UNDERWRITING_COPILOT_MODEL` environment variable.

### Twelve-factor model resolution

Model name resolution follows the twelve-factor precedence
*explicit > environment > hardcoded default*, in
`_resolve_model()`:

```python
def _resolve_model(explicit: str | None) -> str:
    if explicit is not None:
        return explicit
    from_env = os.environ.get(MODEL_ENV_VAR)
    if from_env:
        return from_env
    return DEFAULT_MODEL
```

Resolution is performed at constructor call time, not at module
import time, so the evaluation harness can set the environment
variable mid-process to sweep across models without reloading
modules. This is a small detail but it has implications for how the
eval harness is shaped (Section 5).

---

## The Citation Contract {#sec-method-citation}

The citation contract was committed as part of D013. It comprises
three components: a format, a parser, and a validator.

### Format

Every cited claim is followed by `[chunk_id]` — square brackets
containing the verbatim chunk identifier. The format was chosen for
three reasons: it is simple, it is regex-parseable, and the bracketed
form is unlikely to appear naturally in regulatory text (which
mostly uses parenthetical citations or footnote references).
Alternative forms (`{chunk_id}`, `<cite>...</cite>`, HTML tags) add
parsing complexity without quality benefit.

### Parser

A single regular expression in `src/underwriting_copilot/answer.py`
identifies every citation:

```python
CITATION_REGEX = re.compile(r"\[([A-Za-z0-9_\-]+)\]")
```

The character class `[A-Za-z0-9_\-]` is **strict** — it does not
match spaces, dots, or punctuation. This is deliberate: a naturally-
occurring bracketed phrase in regulatory text like "[Article 12]"
contains a space and a digit, and is therefore not matched. The
parser cannot be tricked into treating prose as a citation.

### Validator: hallucinated-citation detection

After parsing, every extracted `chunk_id` is checked against the
set of chunks that were actually included in the retrieval context:

```python
retrieved_ids = {h.payload["chunk_id"] for h in used_chunks}
cited_ids = set(citation_regex.findall(answer))
hallucinated = cited_ids - retrieved_ids
valid = cited_ids & retrieved_ids
```

Citations that are not in the retrieved set are partitioned into the
`hallucinated_citations` field on the `AnswerResult` and surfaced
explicitly in the UI (as red `[?]` badges in the Streamlit
interface) and in the eval reports. A reviewer cannot mistake a
fabricated citation for a real one — it is structurally labelled as
such at every surface.

This is the load-bearing guardrail against confabulation. Even a
perfectly-formatted citation is worthless if the chunk it points at
was never in the context the model saw, and the structural check is
cheaper than any other validation strategy. It runs in O(citations)
per answer with one set-membership test per citation.

---

## The Refusal Contract {#sec-method-refusal}

The refusal contract is structurally separate from, but
philosophically paired with, the citation contract. Citation
validation catches confabulation when the model *does* answer;
refusal detection catches confabulation when the model *should
not* answer at all.

### The exact phrase

When retrieval surfaces no relevant chunks (or when the model itself
determines the retrieved chunks do not contain the answer), the
system commits to emitting the exact phrase:

```
I cannot answer this from the provided sources.
```

The phrase is **strict-string-matched** against the model's output
after stripping trailing whitespace and end-of-sentence punctuation
(`.`, `!`, `?`). The detector rejects partial refusals — answers
that contain the phrase as a clause but also include other content
("I cannot answer this from the provided sources, but it's commonly
known that…"). These are caught and treated as non-refusals, on the
basis that a partial refusal is worse than no refusal — it gives the
user the impression the system has declined, while still smuggling
in an uncontrolled answer.

### Why exact-match, not semantic similarity

Fuzzy refusal detection (e.g. embedding the model's response and
checking semantic similarity against "I don't know") is more robust
to phrasing drift but introduces ambiguity. Exact-match makes the
detector deterministic and the eval harness's refusal-correctness
metric a clean Boolean. The cost is fragility — the LLM must
produce the exact phrase, and a model with sloppy instruction-
following will fail the contract.

The N=70 sweep shows both candidate models (Gemma and Qwen) honour
the exact-phrase contract in 100% of cases across both prompt
versions. The brittleness is a real cost but not, on this corpus
and these models, a manifest failure mode.

### The refusal is a structured outcome, not an error

`AnswerResult.refused` is a Boolean. When `True`, the
`render_answer_with_badges` path in the UI is replaced by a
dedicated refusal card with a "REFUSAL" label and a brief
explanation of what triggered the refusal (no chunks retrieved, or
chunks retrieved but the model determined them insufficient). The
retrieved chunks (if any) are still shown below the refusal so the
user can verify the decision was sound. This makes refusal a
**first-class outcome**, not a fallback error case.

---

## What Was Deliberately Left Out {#sec-method-deferred}

Three categories of methodology decision were considered and
deferred. They are noted explicitly because deferred decisions are
themselves design decisions:

- **Calibrated retrieval confidence.** The system does not combine
  the four available signals (RRF rank, citation coverage, model
  self-assessment, prompt-fit confidence) into a single confidence
  score. Q3 in `docs/open_questions.md` records both the option and
  the explicit deferral, on the basis that collapsing the four
  signals to one would be misleading at the v1 stage. Each signal
  is reported separately.

- **Cross-encoder reranker.** A second-stage re-ranking pass over
  the fused top-k chunks could plausibly close some of the
  retrieval miss rate observed in the N=70 results. Recorded as
  one of three v2 options under Q13. Not built in v1 because the
  Day-4 diagnostic localised the retrieval gap to single-vector
  dense pooling rather than to fusion noise, which a reranker
  would not address.

- **LLM query rewriting (HyDE-style expansion).** A pre-retrieval
  pass that rewrites or expands the user query before embedding
  could help with the vocabulary-mismatch failures on the
  retrieval miss diagnostic. Also under Q13. Not built in v1 for
  budget reasons; high on the v2 list because it directly targets
  the diagnosed root cause.

The full v2 work-stream is recorded in `docs/open_questions.md`
(Q7, Q13) and surfaces again in Section 8 of this report.

---

## Methodology at a Glance {#sec-method-glance}

| | |
|---|---|
| **Pipeline shape** | 6-stage: retrieve → assemble prompt → LLM → parse citations → detect refusal → return AnswerResult |
| **Retrieval** | Hybrid: BGE-M3 dense + BM25 sparse, fused by RRF (k=60) in Qdrant |
| **Dense embedding** | BGE-M3 1024-dim, CLS-pooled, L2-normalised, via `mlx-embeddings` (D009 + D010) |
| **Sparse retrieval** | BM25 (k1=1.5, b=0.75), word-level Porter-stemmed, ~4,810-term vocab (D011) |
| **Fusion** | Qdrant-native hybrid query, candidates_per_channel = 50, k = 60 |
| **Default top-k** | 5 chunks to the LLM |
| **Filters** | `exclude_superseded` (default True, D012); optional `issuer_type` / `jurisdiction` |
| **LLM endpoint** | `http://127.0.0.1:8000/v1`, oMLX, OpenAI-compatible chat completions |
| **Production model** | `gemma-4-31B-it-MLX-6bit` (D015), overridable via `UNDERWRITING_COPILOT_MODEL` |
| **Temperature** | 0.0, hard-pinned in `_build_payload` |
| **System prompt** | v2 (frozen post-D014); promoted to production by D015 |
| **Citation format** | `[chunk_id]`, regex `\[([A-Za-z0-9_\-]+)\]` |
| **Citation guardrail** | Set-membership check against retrieved chunk_ids; hallucinated citations partitioned into a separate field |
| **Refusal phrase** | "I cannot answer this from the provided sources." — exact strict-string match |
| **Determinism** | Same (query, corpus, model, prompt) → same answer (temperature 0, fixed seeds where models support them) |
| **Streaming** | Not in scope at v1 (single-shot completions only) |
| **Multi-turn** | Not in scope at v1 (each call is independent) |
| **Architectural decisions** | D003 (corpus), D004 (Docling), D006 (metadata), D008 (chunking), D009 (retrieval), D010 (pooling), D011 (BM25), D012 (index), D013 (answer contracts), D014 (eval), D015 (production model) |
